design_philosophy.core_loop: First-person dungeon exploration with turn-based demon battles; player negotiates with demons to recruit them, fuses demons for stronger allies, and progresses through morality-influenced story branches
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Alignment-based narrative divergence combined with demon negotiation RNG and fusion mechanics; player must balance recruitment compatibility with moral choices that reshape the story
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a customizable demon army in a post-apocalyptic Tokyo; negotiate with otherworldly creatures and shape the world's moral destiny through strategic choice-making
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strongly emphasizes player agency through alignment system that affects both story progression and demon recruitment ease; fusion inheritance mechanics reward theorycrafting; demon negotiation depends on personality/moon phase, creating emergent tactical considerations beyond pure stats

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Alignment choice affects demon availability and story difficulty; player can mitigate challenges through careful demon fusion and recruitment strategy; negotiation-based recruitment adds resource management layer

progression.upgrade_system: Demon fusion system where two or three demons combine into predetermined stronger demon types; fused demons inherit abilities from source demons, including non-standard abilities; demon-weapon fusion creates powerful equipment
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story progression unlocks new Tokyo Millennium districts and demon types; alignment choices gate certain demons and story branches; Demon Summoning Program granted mid-game enables core recruitment mechanic
replayability_hooks: Three moral alignments (law/chaos/neutrality) create distinct narrative branches
Demon compatibility with player alignment encourages different recruitment strategies per playthrough
Fusion accident mechanic introduces RNG discovery element
Moon phase affects demon negotiation success, creating temporal resource management

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through post-apocalyptic Tokyo Millennium setting with distinct districts
Character-driven narrative via recurring NPCs (Hiroko, Beth, Daleth, Steven) with conflicting ideologies
Lore exposition through dialogue explaining pre-game apocalypse and religious/political factions
Setting-implied worldbuilding: encapsulated city atmosphere, Messian religion, gladiator tournament structure establish social hierarchy
Protagonist amnesia framework allows player agency in identity discovery

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses represent ideological opponents and alignment-linked antagonists (Daleth as rival Messiah appears multiple times); designed to test demon recruitment strategy and party composition rather than pure execution
notable_bosses: Daleth (recurring rival who claims Messiah status, fights Hawk multiple times)

faction_indicators: Messian religion ruling class in Center district
Law vs. Chaos moral alignment system reflects theological/political ideology
Gladiator tournament in Valhalla as lower-class combat meritocracy
Temple knights as religious military enforcer class
Demon presence in outer districts vs. human-only Center
danger_signals: Religious authority (Bishop) actively reprimanding independent action
Protagonist identity weaponized by religious institution as 'the Messiah'
Mandatory 'eradication' missions against demons suggest systemic persecution
Air quality degradation forcing population concentration under central control
Amnesiac protagonist setup indicates possible identity manipulation
environmental_storytelling: Encapsulated Tokyo Millennium built on Tokyo ruins conveys post-apocalyptic containment
District-based geography reflects class stratification (Valhalla gladiator slums vs. inaccessible Center luxury)
Mutants, fairies, and demons normalized in outer districts while Center remains 'pure'
Arm terminal technology suggests surveillance/control infrastructure
